sanskrit_devanagari:
कर्म ब्रह्मोद्भ‍वं विद्धि ब्रह्माक्षरसमुद्भ‍वम् ।
तस्मात्सर्वगतं ब्रह्म नित्यं यज्ञे प्रतिष्ठितम् ॥ १५ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
karma brahmodbhavaṁ viddhi brahmākṣara-samudbhavam |
tasmāt sarva-gataṁ brahma nityaṁ yajñe pratiṣṭhitam || 15 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: Regulated activities are prescribed in the Vedas, and the Vedas are directly manifested from the Supreme Personality of Godhead. Consequently the all-pervading Transcendence is eternally situated in acts of sacrifice.

Regulated activities are prescribed in the Vedas, and the Vedas are directly manifested from the Supreme Personality of Godhead. Consequently the all-pervading Transcendence is eternally situated in acts of sacrifice.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 2254 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Yajñārtha-karma, or the necessity of work for the satisfaction of Kṛṣṇa only, is more expressly stated in this verse. If we have to work for the satisfaction of the yajña-puruṣa, Viṣṇu, then we must find out the direction of work in Brahman, or the transcendental Vedas.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.15 Again, [a different reading in place of this is: ‘Tat ca vividham karma kuto jatamityaha, From where did those various kinds of action originate? In reply the Lord says…’ Still another reading is: ‘Tat ca karma brahmodbhavam iti aha, And the Lord says: That action has the Vedas as its origin.’-…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-3-15-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The Advaita reading: brahma in this verse = the Veda. karma is born of brahman (the Vedic prescription); brahman is born of the Imperishable (akṣara, the eternal source). Therefore the all-pervading Veda-Brahman is eternally established in yajña. The verse closes the chain into the Veda's authority and into the akṣara's metaphysical primacy. The engineering layer cannot honestly carry this; it must be tagged STRETCHED.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.15 Here ther term, ‘Brahman’ connotes the physical body consisting of modifications of the Prakrti; for the Prakrti is denoted here by the term ‘Brahman’, as in the scriptural text: ‘From Him arises, this Brahman and this ‘Brahman’ becomes name, form and food’ (Mun. U., 1.1.9).
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-3-15-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The Vishishtadvaita reading of brahma here is distinctive: the term refers to the physical body, the modifications of Prakṛti, from which action arises. The reading cites Mun. U. 1.1.9 and Gita 14.3 ('this great Brahman is my womb'). Therefore action is produced by the physical body, which is itself rooted in Prakṛti, which is rooted in the Supreme Person. The verse closes the chain into divine cosmology. The disagreement with the Advaita reading (Veda-as-source vs. Prakṛti-as-source) is doctrinally significant but does not change the engineering-relevant directive: yajña is metaphysically anchored, not optional.

positions.rendering: the Vedas (regulated activities are prescribed in the Vedas, and the Vedas are directly manifested from the Supreme Personality of Godhead)
brahma = the Veda; the Veda is born of the Imperishable; therefore the all-pervading Veda-Brahman is established in yajña
brahma here = the physical body / Prakṛti from which action arises; later (14.3) the Lord says 'this great Brahman is my womb'

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: 3.15's brahma is the chapter's most contested reading. The Advaita reading takes it as the Veda (the textual source from which prescribed action is known), born of the akṣara (Imperishable). The Vishishtadvaita reading takes it as Prakṛti / the body, the source from which action arises. Both preserve: yajña is metaphysically anchored; karma is not free-floating. The disagreement is doctrinally significant (Veda-as-source vs. Prakṛti-as-source) but does not change the verse's directive force. Engineering layers MUST tag this verse STRETCHED — both readings reach metaphysics that the engineering layer cannot honestly carry.

literal_meaning: Know that action is born of Brahman, and Brahman is born of the Imperishable; therefore the all-pervading Brahman is eternally established in yajña.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.15 closes the cosmic chain into Brahman. karma brahmodbhavaṁ viddhi — know that action is born of Brahman; brahmākṣara-samudbhavam — Brahman is born of the Imperishable (akṣara); tasmāt sarva-gataṁ brahma nityaṁ yajñe pratiṣṭhitam — therefore the all-pervading Brahman is eternally established in yajña. Per the Advaita reading: brahma here can mean the Veda (its source is the Imperishable); action arises from the Veda's prescription. Per the Vishishtadvaita reading: brahma here = the body / the Prakṛti from which action arises; yajña is rooted in the cosmic Person. Both readings preserve: yajña is metaphysically anchored, not optional. The verse's cosmological reach is large.
